extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator comprising an insulated styrofoam box, a heatbed for heating, a fan for convection, two internal temperature sensors that monitor the box temperature, one external temperature sensor, and a Raspberry‑Pi based controller that implements a bang‑bang control loop with parameters LL, UL, H and C. The system aims to maintain a target temperature regardless of load content and operates in a laboratory or industrial incubation environment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides the following services:
1. **Real‑time visualization** – streams the controller and low‑level driver messages to a time‑series database and renders them on a dashboard, allowing operators to monitor incubator status live.
2. **On‑demand calibration** – automatically triggers the calibration routine described in Section 3.3 to update model parameters when new data are available.
3. **Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation** – runs a Kalman filter that uses Model B to infer the non‑measured heat‑bed temperature and detect deviations from expected behaviour.
4. **What‑if simulations** – performs co‑simulations that feed historical or real‑time PT data into the twin, enabling optimisation of controller parameters (e.g., as shown in Figure 8).
5. **Self‑adaptation loop** – upon detection of a behavioural change (e.g., due to a newly placed object), the twin schedules experiments, recalibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, runs optimisation simulations, and re‑configures the controller—thus closing the feedback loop.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on a Raspberry Pi as the embedded controller interfacing with a heatbed, fan, and three temperature sensors. Communication between the physical devices and the DT is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker acting as the communication server. A low‑level driver abstracts sensor/actuator interactions and publishes sensor readings to RabbitMQ, while the controller consumes control commands and publishes control states. The complete source code, hardware specifications, and experimental datasets are publicly hosted in a GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The low‑level driver in the incubator continuously polls the two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor, publishing the read values to RabbitMQ. The controller consumes these messages and executes a bang‑bang control state machine defined in Figure 8. The control state machine uses two timing parameters – H (heating duration) and C (waiting duration) – that determine how long the heatbed is powered on and how long the system waits before re‑reading the temperature. The system’s temporal granularity is therefore dictated by the sensor polling interval and the H/C cycle durations. No explicit synchronisation frequency, latency bounds, or other time‑scale requirements are specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture employs a single, centralized Digital Twin instance that encapsulates the entire incubator. It contains two distinct physical‑model representations (Model A and Model B) and one control‑logic component (the bang‑bang controller). No distributed or hierarchical twin instances are instantiated, but responsibilities are clearly partitioned among the physical twin, the low‑level driver, the controller, and the DT analytics services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin constellation is orchestrated through a lightweight, message‑broker based architecture. The low‑level driver reads sensor values and writes actuator commands to a RabbitMQ server. RabbitMQ then forwards the data to the controller, which in turn issues HVAC commands and publishes its state back to RabbitMQ. In the conceptual DT layer, data streams from the PT are ingested and processed by the model instances (Model A and Model B) and by algorithmic services (e.g., calibration routines, Kalman‑filter state estimation). All components share the same broker and operate in a tightly coupled, real‑time manner, but there is no explicit separation of concerns beyond the logical division between data acquisition, control, modelling, and analytics. The DT does not mention any separate service for Kalman filtering; it is treated as part of the analytics algorithms rather than an independent module.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is primarily achieved through RabbitMQ, an open‑source AMQP message broker. All Digital Twin components publish and subscribe to messages via RabbitMQ, allowing inter‑component communication without requiring explicit API gateways or REST endpoints. The system relies on standard message queues and topics (e.g., controller‑to‑driver, driver‑to‑controller), adhering to AMQP messaging semantics. No other external integration protocols or enterprise systems are referenced in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin uses a mix of geometric, behavioral and analytical models.  A CAD model of the incubator provides geometric data (sizes of the box, heatbed, fan mounting).  Two behavioral models are used: Model A (simplified, 2 free parameters) and Model B (more detailed, 4 free parameters) that predict the internal temperature dynamics via differential equations derived from heat‑balance considerations.  Data is sourced from two temperature sensors inside the box and one external sensor, collected continuously by a low‑level driver and transmitted through a RabbitMQ server to the Digital Twin layer.  Calibration algorithms update the model parameters with online measurements; the resulting data feeds a dashboard for real‑time visualization and a Kalman‑filter based anomaly detector.  Model relationships are hierarchical: the geometric model defines the physical layout, the behavioral models instantiate the physics and cyber dynamics, and the analytical equations bridge them.  Their specific roles are simulation (predictive behavior), monitoring (status checks), optimization (controller tuning) and decision support (what‑if studies).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuators consist of a heat‑bed and a fan inside the incubator. The heat‑bed is the primary heating element and its on/off state is controlled by a bang‑bang controller; actuation is defined by *H* (heating duration) and *C* (waiting duration) parameters. The fan distributes heat and is commanded via the same control channel. Remote control is achieved through a RabbitMQ messaging server where the controller publishes actuation commands and receives sensor data. Actuation ranges are limited by the thermostat settings (lower limit *LL* and upper limit *UL* of temperature). Safety constraints enforce that the heat‑bed cannot exceed a set temperature threshold to avoid overheating; once the temperature reaches *UL*, the controller turns the heat‑bed off. The addressing mechanism is implicit in the message routing of RabbitMQ (queues for heatbed, fan, and sensor updates).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The incubator houses three high‑accuracy temperature sensors: two placed inside the insulated box to monitor the process atmosphere, and one external sensor for ambient temperature. Sensor accuracy is specified at ±0.5 °C, ensuring reliable temperature readings for model calibration and anomaly detection. Sensors are polled periodically by the low‑level driver, which uploads the data to the RabbitMQ communication server. The data volume is small, but the messages are timestamped and queued for ingestion into the digital twin’s time‑series datastore.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed through calibration and experimental validation: model B is preferred because it captures the thermal inertia of the heatbed.  Validation experiments compare model predictions with measured temperature traces, and calibration updates free parameters.  Uncertainty sources are the simplifying assumptions (e.g., uniform temperature, negligible wall heat, constant specific heat, ignored fan heat).  Anomaly detection uses a Kalman filter to flag deviations; errors are corrected by re‑calibration.  Quality assurance involves real‑time dashboards, logged sensor data, and systematic comparison of predicted versus actual temperatures during controlled ramp tests.  The process aligns with industry practices of iterative calibration, validation, and what‑if simulation to maintain model validity over time.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Physical Twin (PT) consists of three temperature sensors (two inside a styrofoam insulated box and one external). Data is read periodically by the low‑level driver running on the Raspberry Pi. Each measurement (temperature in °C) is packaged into a message and published to the RabbitMQ communication server, which acts as the Digital Twin’s data ingestion point. The low‑level driver also implements a safe protocol that guarantees the device remains in a safe state before any data is accepted or forwarded, thus ensuring basic data quality assurance. The DT components (calibration algorithm, monitors, dashboards) subscribe to the RabbitMQ topic to receive the live sensor telemetry, integrating it into their models and visualizations.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The controller implements a bang‑bang logic that receives the latest temperature measurements from the broker, evaluates them against configured lower (LL) and upper (UL) limits, and sends control commands to the low‑level driver through the same RabbitMQ broker. The driver validates each command against its safe protocol, which guarantees that no actuators (fan or heatbed) will be driven beyond safe thresholds, and translates the high‑level commands into hardware‑specific signals (e.g. GPIO toggles). After execution, the driver reports its state back to the broker, allowing the controller to confirm command completion and adjust the next cycle accordingly.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All DT communication is mediated by RabbitMQ (an AMQP 0-9-1 compliant broker) running locally on the Raspberry Pi. Messages flow between the controller, the low‑level driver and any DT components that subscribe to the broker. The network topology is a simple star: all nodes connect to the local RabbitMQ server over the LAN. The use of AMQP ensures reliable, acknowledged message delivery without requiring external network access. No specific bandwidth or latency figures are specified, but the architecture supports the low‑frequency periodic temperature updates and control commands required by the system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The entire Digital Twin stack is hosted on‑premises, with all software components (controller, low‑level driver and RabbitMQ broker) running on the same Raspberry Pi that powers the incubator. The repository provides the source code and configuration scripts for local deployment; the DT can also be extended by running additional subscriber processes on the Pi or on other in‑house machines. No cloud hosting or edge servers are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The digital twin engineering process for the incubator proceeds through a sequence of stages that begin with physical twin hardware construction, followed by the development of two simulation models (Model A with four free parameters and Model B with two free parameters). The models are calibrated against real‑time sensor data collected by two internal temperature sensors and one external sensor. Calibration employs a least‑squares parameter‑identification routine that adjusts the free parameters of each model to match the measured temperature profiles. Updated model parameters are packaged as configuration files and loaded into the simulation engine that runs alongside the controller. The controller, implemented on a Raspberry Pi, executes a bang‑bang control loop with defined lower (LL), upper (UL), heating (H), and waiting (C) limits. Real‑time data and commands traverse the system via RabbitMQ, a lightweight message‑broker, allowing the digital twin to ingest sensory streams, query the simulation engine, and feed back control suggestions.

All source code, model definitions, calibration scripts, and documentation are published in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator). This repository provides version control and enables continuous integration: automated unit tests run against the controller and simulation scripts, while integration tests trigger a full calibration cycle on a schedule, updating the model files and redeploying the digital twin components. The circulation of sensor data and model updates is logged, and a model registry records the version and performance metrics of each calibration run, supporting governance and traceability of the twin evolution.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The incubator digital twin generates actionable insights by (1) providing real‑time visualization: messages from the controller and low‑level driver are consumed through RabbitMQ, stored in a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB), and plotted on a dashboard web interface; (2) performing anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation via a Kalman filter that fuses Model B predictions with the two internal sensor readings, yielding an estimate of the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and associated confidence bounds; (3) executing on‑demand calibration: whenever a new object is introduced, the DT automatically triggers a calibration experiment, re‑calculates model parameters, and updates the controller configuration; (4) enabling what‑if simulations: a co‑simulation engine injects historical or live PT data into the simulation model to evaluate alternative controller settings and optimize performance; (5) implementing a self‑adaptation loop: Kalman‑filter anomalies trigger a sequence of actions—schedule experiment, re‑run calibration, update controller, re‑optimize controller parameters—before resuming normal operation. These capabilities support users via a graphical UI and expose confidence measures (e.g., Kalman‑filter covariance) to guide decision making.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Incubator’s Digital Twin implementation uses a safe protocol within the low‑level driver to keep the device in a safe state. The controller employs a bang‑bang control strategy with defined lower/upper temperature limits (LL, UL), heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C) to prevent temperature overshoot. Communication between the Raspberry Pi controller and the low‑level driver is routed through a RabbitMQ message broker, chosen for its lightweight, mature, and easy deployment in on‑premise and cloud environments. No explicit cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, compliance, or data‑sharing agreements are described in the documents.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
